levers.name: Platform-Neutral Access Requirements
levers.consequences: Implementing platform-neutral access requirements would directly reduce Denmark's dependence on Apple iOS and Google Android for digital identity services, mitigating the risk of supplier concentration. However, this might lead to increased complexity in system development and maintenance.
options: Embed platform-neutrality as a non-negotiable requirement in all future AltID procurements
Develop a standards-based, open-source alternative to MitID that can be deployed on any device or platform
Establish a certification program for digital identity apps that meet platform-neutral access standards
levers.review_lever: Controls supplier concentration vs. increased system complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of implementing and enforcing strict platform-neutrality requirements across all future procurements.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Path
levers.consequences: Implementing a fallback authentication path would provide an alternative means of authentication in case of platform failures or changes, enhancing resilience. However, this might increase costs and complexity.
options: Develop a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication system that can be used on any device
Implement a decentralized identity management system using blockchain technology
Create a standards-based API for fallback authentication that can be integrated into existing systems
levers.review_lever: Controls resilience vs. increased costs and complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of implementing and integrating a fallback authentication path with existing digital identity systems.

levers.name: Procurement Intervention
levers.consequences: Influencing procurement processes would directly impact the development and deployment of digital identity services, potentially reducing dependence on foreign technology suppliers. However, this might lead to resistance from existing operators.
options: Develop a set of procurement guidelines that prioritize platform-neutrality and resilience
Establish a public-private partnership model for digital identity service development and deployment
Create a government-led initiative to promote the adoption of open-source and platform-neutral technologies
levers.review_lever: Controls supplier concentration vs. resistance from existing operators. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of influencing procurement processes and ensuring compliance with new guidelines.

levers.name: Standards Pressure
levers.consequences: Applying standards pressure would influence the development and deployment of digital identity services, potentially driving adoption of platform-neutral and resilient technologies. However, this might lead to increased complexity in system development and maintenance.
options: Develop a set of industry-wide standards for digital identity service development and deployment
Establish a certification program for digital identity apps that meet platform-neutral access standards
Create a public-private partnership model to promote the adoption of open-source and platform-neutral technologies
levers.review_lever: Controls complexity vs. increased complexity in system development and maintenance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of implementing and enforcing industry-wide standards across all relevant stakeholders.

levers.name: Credible Technical Demonstration
levers.consequences: Developing a credible technical demonstration would provide evidence for the feasibility of platform-neutral and resilient digital identity services, potentially driving adoption. However, this might lead to increased costs and complexity.
options: Develop a non-production demonstrator that showcases a platform-neutral authentication path using open standards
Create a proof-of-concept prototype for a decentralized identity management system using blockchain technology
Establish a public-private partnership model to promote the adoption of open-source and platform-neutral technologies
levers.review_lever: Controls costs vs. increased complexity in system development and maintenance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of developing and deploying a credible technical demonstration that meets industry standards.

levers.name: Regulatory and Compliance Framework
levers.consequences: Establishing a regulatory and compliance framework would provide a clear set of rules and guidelines for digital identity service development and deployment, potentially reducing dependence on foreign technology suppliers. However, this might lead to increased complexity in system development and maintenance.
options: Develop a set of industry-wide regulations for digital identity service development and deployment
Establish a certification program for digital identity apps that meet platform-neutral access standards
Create a public-private partnership model to promote the adoption of open-source and platform-neutral technologies
levers.review_lever: Controls complexity vs. increased complexity in system development and maintenance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of implementing and enforcing industry-wide regulations across all relevant stakeholders.

levers.name: Institutional Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging with institutional stakeholders would provide a clear understanding of their needs and priorities, potentially driving adoption of platform-neutral and resilient digital identity services. However, this might lead to increased complexity in system development and maintenance.
options: Establish a public-private partnership model to promote the adoption of open-source and platform-neutral technologies
Develop a set of industry-wide standards for digital identity service development and deployment
Create a certification program for digital identity apps that meet platform-neutral access standards
levers.review_lever: Controls complexity vs. increased complexity in system development and maintenance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of engaging with institutional stakeholders and ensuring their buy-in.

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging at the EU level would provide a clear understanding of broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. However, this might lead to increased complexity in system development and maintenance.
options: Establish a public-private partnership model to promote the adoption of open-source and platform-neutral technologies
Develop a set of industry-wide standards for digital identity service development and deployment
Create a certification program for digital identity apps that meet platform-neutral access standards
levers.review_lever: Controls complexity vs. increased complexity in system development and maintenance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of engaging at the EU level and ensuring compliance with broader European requirements.

responses.summary: Add 'Standards-based procurement guidelines' to Procurement Intervention lever

levers.name: Interoperability Push
levers.consequences: Direct effect: The Danish government pressures the EU to include platform-neutral access requirements in the European Digital Identity Wallet framework. Downstream implication: This could lead to a coordinated effort among member states to reduce dependence on Apple and Google, but might also face resistance from existing operators.
options: Drive EU-level policy changes through coalition building
Emphasize interoperability for critical public services
Develop a 'Digital Identity Passport' concept
levers.review_lever: Controls platform-neutral access vs. exclusive reliance on Apple/Google. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact of incumbent resistance from existing operators.

levers.name: Public-Private Partnership
levers.consequences: Direct effect: A public-private partnership is formed to develop and deploy a platform-neutral digital identity solution. Downstream implication: This might lead to increased investment in digital infrastructure, but could also raise concerns about data privacy and security.
options: Establish a joint venture between the Danish government and private sector companies
Develop a 'Digital Identity Hub' with multiple stakeholders
Create an open-source platform for digital identity
levers.review_lever: Controls public-private collaboration vs. exclusive reliance on government resources. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential risks of data sharing and security breaches.

levers.name: Regulatory Incentives
levers.consequences: Direct effect: The Danish government introduces regulatory incentives for companies to adopt platform-neutral digital identity solutions. Downstream implication: This could lead to increased adoption rates, but might also create a competitive advantage for larger companies.
options: Offer tax breaks or subsidies for companies using platform-neutral solutions
Create a 'Digital Identity Certification' program
Develop a 'Digital Identity Framework' for regulatory guidance
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory incentives vs. market forces. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs).

levers.name: Stakeholder Engagement
levers.consequences: Direct effect: A broad coalition of stakeholders is engaged in the development and deployment of a platform-neutral digital identity solution. Downstream implication: This might lead to increased buy-in from various groups, but could also create conflicting interests.
options: Establish a 'Digital Identity Council' with representatives from government, industry, and civil society
Develop a 'Stakeholder Engagement Framework' for inclusive decision-making
Create an online platform for stakeholder feedback and discussion
levers.review_lever: Controls stakeholder engagement vs. exclusive reliance on government decisions. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential risks of conflicting interests and power imbalances.

levers.name: Technical Demonstrators
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Technical demonstrators are developed to showcase the feasibility of platform-neutral digital identity solutions. Downstream implication: This could lead to increased confidence in the technology, but might also create unrealistic expectations.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator using open standards
Create a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path
Design an 'Identity-as-a-Service' platform for scalability and flexibility
levers.review_lever: Controls technical demonstrators vs. focus on policy changes. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential risks of over-reliance on technology.

levers.name: Procurement Reforms
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Procurement reforms are introduced to favor platform-neutral digital identity solutions. Downstream implication: This could lead to increased adoption rates, but might also create a competitive advantage for larger companies.
options: Develop a 'Digital Identity Procurement Framework' for government agencies
Create a 'Procurement Incentives Program' for companies using platform-neutral solutions
Establish a 'Digital Identity Marketplace' for secure and transparent transactions
levers.review_lever: Controls procurement reforms vs. market forces. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential impact on SMEs.

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement
levers.consequences: Direct effect: The Danish government engages with EU bodies and technical working groups to promote platform-neutral digital identity solutions. Downstream implication: This could lead to increased coordination among member states, but might also face resistance from existing operators.
options: Drive EU-level policy changes through coalition building
Emphasize interoperability for critical public services
Develop a 'Digital Identity Passport' concept
levers.review_lever: Controls EU-level engagement vs. exclusive reliance on national policies. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential risks of conflicting interests and power imbalances.

responses.summary: Add 'Transparency and Accountability Mechanisms' to Stakeholder Engagement, to ensure that all stakeholders are held accountable for their actions and decisions.

levers.name: Supply Chain Risk Profiling
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Identify specific risks associated with relying on Apple iOS and Google Android for Danish digital identity access. Downstream implication: This profiling will inform procurement decisions, ensuring the selection of suppliers that align with Denmark's digital sovereignty goals.
options: Conduct a comprehensive risk assessment of current supplier concentration, focusing on potential security vulnerabilities and continuity-of-service exposure
Develop a detailed analysis of MitID's dependency on Apple/Google platform integrity signals, highlighting the consequences of vendor lock-in
Create a supply chain risk matrix to evaluate and prioritize mitigation strategies for identified risks
levers.review_lever: Controls supplier concentration vs. security vulnerabilities. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of existing relationships with Apple and Google on future procurement decisions.

levers.name: Digital Identity Ecosystem Mapping
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Visualize the complex relationships between MitID, AltID, and other digital identity stakeholders. Downstream implication: This mapping will facilitate a deeper understanding of the current ecosystem's dynamics, enabling more effective intervention strategies.
options: Develop an interactive map illustrating the various connections between MitID, AltID, and related digital identity services
Create a detailed graph showing the dependencies between different components of Denmark's digital identity infrastructure
Produce a visual representation of the relationships between key stakeholders in the Danish digital identity ecosystem
levers.review_lever: Controls digital identity landscape vs. stakeholder interactions. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for mapping errors or outdated information.

levers.name: Stakeholder Engagement Framework
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Establish a structured approach to engaging with diverse stakeholders, including civil society organizations and government bodies. Downstream implication: This framework will facilitate more effective coalition-building and advocacy efforts.
options: Develop a stakeholder engagement plan that prioritizes building relationships with key organizations and individuals in the digital identity space
Create a matrix outlining the different communication channels and strategies for engaging with various stakeholders, including government officials and industry leaders
Establish a clear framework for evaluating the effectiveness of stakeholder engagement efforts
levers.review_lever: Controls stakeholder relationships vs. advocacy efforts. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for over-reliance on existing networks or relationships.

levers.name: Procurement Policy Reform
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Influence procurement decisions to prioritize digital sovereignty and platform-neutral access requirements. Downstream implication: This reform will ensure that Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is built with resilience and continuity in mind.
options: Advocate for the inclusion of digital sovereignty and platform-neutrality language in official procurement documents
Develop a detailed analysis of current procurement practices, highlighting areas where reforms can be implemented to support Denmark's digital sovereignty goals
Create a proposal outlining a new procurement framework that prioritizes supplier diversity and resilience
levers.review_lever: Controls procurement policies vs. supplier relationships. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for resistance from existing suppliers or industry leaders.

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Establish connections with EU bodies and technical working groups related to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework. Downstream implication: This engagement will inform Denmark's advocacy efforts, ensuring alignment with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in.
options: Develop a detailed analysis of current EU policies and initiatives relevant to digital identity and sovereignty
Establish relationships with key EU stakeholders, including members of the European Parliament and technical working groups
Create a proposal outlining Denmark's advocacy goals and strategies for influencing EU-level policy decisions
levers.review_lever: Controls EU relationships vs. policy influence. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for bureaucratic delays or resistance from existing EU stakeholders.

levers.name: Regulatory Clarity
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Provide clear guidance on regulatory requirements and standards for digital identity infrastructure in Denmark. Downstream implication: This clarity will ensure that industry leaders and stakeholders understand the expected standards of practice.
options: Develop a detailed analysis of current regulations and standards relevant to digital identity and sovereignty
Create a clear and concise guide outlining regulatory requirements and expectations for industry leaders and stakeholders
Establish relationships with key regulatory bodies, ensuring alignment between Denmark's advocacy efforts and EU-level policies
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory environment vs. compliance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for regulatory ambiguity or conflicting priorities.

levers.name: Institutional Overhaul
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Reform institutional structures and processes to prioritize digital sovereignty and platform-neutral access requirements. Downstream implication: This overhaul will ensure that Denmark's institutions are equipped to support the country's digital sovereignty goals.
options: Develop a detailed analysis of current institutional structures and processes, highlighting areas where reforms can be implemented to support digital sovereignty
Create a proposal outlining a new institutional framework that prioritizes supplier diversity and resilience
Establish relationships with key stakeholders, including government officials and industry leaders, to ensure alignment between Denmark's advocacy efforts and EU-level policies
levers.review_lever: Controls institutional structures vs. policy influence. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for resistance from existing institutions or industry leaders.

levers.name: Public-Private Partnership
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Establish partnerships between government bodies and private sector organizations to drive digital identity innovation. Downstream implication: This partnership will ensure that Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is developed with the input of industry leaders and stakeholders.
options: Develop a detailed analysis of current public-private partnerships relevant to digital identity and sovereignty
Create a proposal outlining a new public-private partnership framework that prioritizes supplier diversity and resilience
Establish relationships with key private sector organizations, ensuring alignment between Denmark's advocacy efforts and EU-level policies
levers.review_lever: Controls public-private relationships vs. innovation. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for conflicts of interest or unequal power dynamics.

levers.name: Digital Identity Standards
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Establish clear standards and guidelines for digital identity infrastructure in Denmark. Downstream implication: This standardization will ensure that industry leaders and stakeholders understand the expected standards of practice.
options: Develop a detailed analysis of current digital identity standards relevant to sovereignty and platform-neutrality
Create a clear and concise guide outlining regulatory requirements and expectations for industry leaders and stakeholders
Establish relationships with key stakeholders, including government officials and industry leaders, to ensure alignment between Denmark's advocacy efforts and EU-level policies
levers.review_lever: Controls digital identity standards vs. compliance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for conflicting priorities or regulatory ambiguity.

levers.name: Cybersecurity Framework
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Establish a clear framework for cybersecurity practices and protocols in Denmark's digital identity infrastructure. Downstream implication: This framework will ensure that industry leaders and stakeholders understand the expected standards of practice.
options: Develop a detailed analysis of current cybersecurity frameworks relevant to sovereignty and platform-neutrality
Create a clear and concise guide outlining regulatory requirements and expectations for industry leaders and stakeholders
Establish relationships with key stakeholders, including government officials and industry leaders, to ensure alignment between Denmark's advocacy efforts and EU-level policies
levers.review_lever: Controls cybersecurity framework vs. compliance. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for conflicting priorities or regulatory ambiguity.

responses.summary: Add 'a detailed analysis of current digital identity standards' to Digital Identity Standards

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

Today's date:
2026-Mar-08

Project start ASAP

It requires a physical base in Copenhagen for research, development, and stakeholder meetings. The project involves building demonstrators, engaging with government bodies, and influencing procurement processes, all of which necessitate physical presence and interaction. The project also requires a physical location with access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings.
